name: Two Pointers (in-place)
summary: Put one pointer at each end and swap the two characters, then step both pointers inward until they meet. Reverses in place with no extra array.
visualizationIntent: init: characters shown as a row of cells; left pointer at index 0, right pointer at the last index. loop: while left < right. update (L6): the two end cells (current) swap their characters. move (L7-8): both pointers step inward; the just-swapped outer cells lock to result. return: left and right have crossed — the whole row is reversed (all result).

lineExplanations.3: Place the left pointer at the first character.
lineExplanations.4: Place the right pointer at the last character.
lineExplanations.5: While the two pointers haven't met, there is still a pair to swap.
lineExplanations.6: Swap the characters at the two ends in a single step.
lineExplanations.7: Move the left pointer one step toward the middle.
lineExplanations.8: Move the right pointer one step toward the middle.
lineExplanations.9: The pointers met — return the array, now reversed in place.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: — the class wrapper the platform expects your method to live inside.
syntaxExplanations.2: def reverseString(self, s): — defines the method; s is the list of characters.
syntaxExplanations.3: left = 0 — index of the first character.
syntaxExplanations.4: right = len(s) - 1 — index of the last character.
syntaxExplanations.5: while left < right: — keep going until the two pointers meet in the middle.
syntaxExplanations.6: s[left], s[right] = s[right], s[left] — Python tuple swap: exchange the two characters at once.
syntaxExplanations.7: left += 1 — move the left pointer one step right.
syntaxExplanations.8: right -= 1 — move the right pointer one step left.
syntaxExplanations.9: return s — the same array, now reversed in place.
